title: Albertine
description: A Norwegian realist novel attacking sexual exploitation, prostitution, and official hypocrisy.
descriptionParagraphs: A Norwegian realist novel attacking sexual exploitation, prostitution, and official hypocrisy.

Albertine is usually read through its treatment of sexuality, social reform, and realism. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes sexuality, social reform, and realism feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A Norwegian realist novel attacking sexual exploitation, prostitution, and official hypocrisy.
banningParagraphs: Albertine entered censorship debates as a novel associated with sexuality, social reform, and realism. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and public morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1886 in Norway, where Norwegian authorities confiscated and banned. The book's realism about prostitution and abuse triggered confiscation soon after release. It is a foundational Scandinavian censorship case.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
